base_case: Cepeda v. Cumberland Engineering Co., 76 N.J. 152 (1978)
doctrinal_framework_source: Wade seven-factor risk-utility test (New Jersey adoption)
constructor: Venkat Porto (drafted by Claude Opus stand-in; auto-generated from base instance)
case_background: Plaintiff Cepeda, a factory worker, operated a pelletizing machine manufactured by Cumberland Engineering. The machine had a point-of-operation hazard — the intake port where plastic feedstock is pushed into rollers for pellet formation — and Cepeda's fingers were drawn into the rollers and amputated. He sued Cumberland on design-defect grounds, arguing that an interlock guard preventing hand insertion into the intake was a feasible safer design. The New Jersey Supreme Court adopted the seven Wade risk-utility factors and remanded for jury consideration of design defect under the new test.
